content.problemStatement:
In a recent breakthrough, sequences which do not have Arithmetic Progressions (APs) of size 3, were studied. Let us try a simple problem related to that.

Consider a sequence A1≤A2≤…≤AnA_1 \leq A_2 \leq \ldots \leq A_nA1​≤A2​≤…≤An​. Three elements from it, say Ai,Aj,AkA_i, A_j, A_kAi​,Aj​,Ak​, where i<j<ki < j < ki<j<k, are said to form an AP, if (Aj−Ai)=(Ak−Aj)(A_j - A_i) = (A_k - A_j)(Aj​−Ai​)=(Ak​−Aj​). Call the sequence **AP-Free**, if no such three elements can be found.

Your goal is to find out whether the given sequence is **AP-Free** or not.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the size of the sequence.
The next line contains NNN integers - A1,A2,…,AnA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_nA1​,A2​,…,An​, which is the sequence.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, "YES", if the sequence is **AP-Free**. Output "NO" if it is not.


You may print each character in uppercase or lowercase. For example, `Yes`, `yes`, `YES`, and `yES` are all considered identical.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N (the length of the sequence). The second line contains N integers A1 … AN in non‑decreasing order.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if the sequence is AP‑Free, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Testcase 1: (1,2,3) forms an AP → not AP‑Free → NO.
Testcase 2: No three numbers have equal consecutive differences → AP‑Free → YES.
Testcase 3: (10,25,40) is an AP → NO.
Testcase 4: (1,11,21) is an AP → NO.

hidden.label: edge case with duplicates but AP‑Free

solution.approach: For each test case we need to know whether there exist indices i < j < k such that Aj - Ai = Ak - Aj. Because the array is sorted we can treat Aj as the middle element. For every possible pair (i, k) with i < k we compute the value that should be in the middle: mid = (Ai + Ak) / 2. If Ai + Ak is even we binary‑search the sub‑array (i+1 … k-1) for mid. Finding any such triple means the sequence is NOT AP‑Free. If no pair yields a valid middle element the sequence is AP‑Free. The double loop runs O(N^2) and each binary search is O(log N), which is easily fast for N ≤ 100.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2 log N) per test case (worst case 100^2 * log 100 ≈ 10^4 operations).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra besides the input array.
